Title: Estee Lauder taps JP Morgan to finance Puig takeover bid
Article:
MADRID: US cosmetics giant Estee Lauder has commissioned JP Morgan to structure a financing package of around five billion euros (US$5.89 billion) for its takeover bid of Spanish firm Puig, sources told Spanish newspaper Expansion.
Last month, Puig and Estee Lauder said they were exploring a deal that would create the world's largest premium beauty player, with brands including Tom Ford, Carolina Herrera, Rabanne, Jean Paul Gaultier, and Clinique under one roof.
Estee Lauder did not immediately reply to a Reuters' request for comment outside of US business hours.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 23 compounds issued in KLCC 'ulat foto' operation
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) has stepped up enforcement against unauthorised photography activities around KLCC, with 28 items seized and 23 compounds issued under Op Lens Series 6/2026.
In a Facebook post, DBKL said the operation was jointly conducted with police, the Immigration Department and the National Registration Department (NRD) on Sunday, targeting "ulat foto" (photo touts) activities in public areas.
In the latest operation, DBKL issued four compounds and seized three sets of equipment, including mobile phones.
Overall, authorities recorded 28 seizures and issued 23 compounds, involving seven non-citizens.
Four people were also issued notices to attend court after failing to settle their compounds.
The operation was conducted under the Licensing of Hawkers (Federal Territory of Kuala Lumpur) By-Laws 2016.
Separately, the departments' officers inspected 31 people during the operation.
Four were issued notices of document retention under Section 21(1)(c) of the National Registration Regulations 1990 (Amendment 2007) under the National Registration Act 1959 for further verification of identity documents and citizenship status.
DBKL said enforcement will continue to curb illegal photography activities that pose safety risks, cause public nuisance and disrupt traffic flow in the city centre.
"All seized items have been taken to the DBKL seizure store for documentation and record purposes," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK wage growth cools by less than expected in three months to February
Article:
LONDON: British wage growth, excluding bonuses, slowed by slightly less than expected to 3.6 per cent in annual terms in the three months to February, the Office for National Statistics said on Tuesday.
Economists polled by Reuters had mostly expected regular average weekly earnings growth of 3.5 per cent, down from 3.8 per cent in the previous month.
Until the outbreak of the Iran war at the end of February, the Bank of England had been showing increasing concern about weaker hiring.
But new inflation pressures have emerged, caused by the jump in energy prices due to the war in the Middle East, and economists have pushed back their forecasts for when the BoE is likely resume cutting borrowing costs.
Officials at the central bank say the weak economic backdrop is likely to make it harder for workers to demand bigger pay increases in response to the expected rise in inflation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Matrade sticks to 3.0–5.0pct trade growth forecast despite geopolitical tensions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia External Trade Development Corporation (Matrade) is maintaining its 2026 export and trade growth projection at three to five per cent despite ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.
Matrade chairman Datuk Seri Reezal Merican Naina Merican, however, said the forecast may be revised if conditions worsen or escalate into a global recession.
"We forecast that our export growth and trade growth this year will be in the region of between three and five per cent. In a modest way, I want to still remain that as a figure.
"That said, there are many unforeseen risks ahead. The current situation could escalate beyond an energy crisis into a global recession. If it is a global recession, it will not affect a country, it will affect the entire country.
"If we have people who are becoming less capable and less equipped, chances are that that country may not be able to buy that product. It will impact our exports. But as for now, we are still looking at the same figure," he said.
Reezal was speaking at Matrade's first-quarter trade update press conference and briefing on strategic measures to help exporters mitigate risks.
He said March's performance was unexpectedly strong, providing a useful gauge of how conditions may evolve in the coming months.
Malaysia recorded its highest ever trade in the first quarter of a year in Q1 2026, with total trade reaching RM789.85 billion despite the ongoing US-Iran war.
Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry on Monday said the RM789.85 billion in trade in Q1 2026 was a 10 per cent increase from trade recorded in the same quarter last year.
Exports rose 12.7 per cent year-on-year to RM426.53 billion, the second-highest recorded in a quarter, while imports increased by 7.7 per cent to RM363.31 billion.
Reezal encouraged exporters to explore other promising industries where Malaysia has strengths, if not globally then at least regionally, such as pharmaceuticals.
"Pharmaceuticals and medical devices are among Malaysia's top strengths in Asean, but many of the manufacturing plants in this sector are owned by multinational companies.
"We want to see a future where, beyond electrical and electronics dominating our exports and trade, other high-potential sectors also play a larger role such as pharmaceuticals, medical devices, energy and food and beverage (F&B). At present, F&B accounts for only about 1.8-1.9 per cent of total exports," he added.
Reezal said as Malaysia positions itself as a leading promoter of the halal industry, it should capitalise on the global halal market, which is now valued at about US$3.5 trillion, to better leverage its current standing and reap the associated benefits.
Matrade also unveiled a comprehensive strategic mitigation plan aimed at protecting Malaysian exporters from rising global trade tensions and uncertainty.
Reezal said the objective is to shift the national approach from merely recognising the impact of crises to adopting a more proactive facilitation strategy.
He added that through intelligence sharing, digital innovation and targeted government support, Malaysia aims to safeguard exporters from global shocks while sustaining the country's trade momentum and minimising the impact of disruptions.
"We are intensifying our monitoring by leveraging our 47 overseas offices as 'Frontline Intelligence Hubs' to provide exporters with real-time data on port connectivity and tactical routing.
"By evolving the role of our trade commissioners to identify the supply gaps arising from geopolitical tension and sourcing of alternative materials, we are keeping Malaysian industries running even when traditional supply lines are under strain," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia targets 32pct installed renewable energy capacity this year
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is committed to reaching 32 per cent installed renewable energy (RE) capacity this year, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said.
Fadillah, who is also the Minister of Energy Transition and Water Transformation, said the country had achieved 31 per cent installed RE capacity as of December 2025.
"The government aims to increase installed renewable energy capacity by one per cent a year until we reach 35 per cent by 2030 or earlier.
"The RE mix consists of many different sources such as solar, biogas and biofuels. InsyaAllah, we will achieve this through all the programmes we have introduced, including battery storage and so on," he told Bernama after appearing as a guest on Bernama Radio's exclusive interview titled "Geopolitical Crisis Drives Malaysia's Energy Transition" at Wisma Bernama today.
Under the National Energy Transition Roadmap (NETR), Malaysia aims to reach 70 per cent RE capacity by 2050.
Fadillah said that currently about 40 to 50 per cent of the country's energy use is based on gas, and although much still relies on carbon-based sources, the country is committed to no longer using coal by 2044.
"That is why we are now introducing a lot of large-scale solar and rooftop solar to encourage more use of renewable energy. InsyaAllah, this year we will also open another tender for the sixth large-scale solar project (LSS 6)," he said.
He said that, besides solar, Malaysia is looking at a mix of other energy sources, including biodiesel, biogas, and others, to ensure the country has a sufficient energy supply in the future.
"For biodiesel, we have agreed to increase the blend of palm oil in diesel to 12 per cent. In addition, biogas can actually be produced from palm oil as well," he said.
Fadillah said Malaysia is also studying the potential of using nuclear power.
He said studies have been carried out, and the country now needs to enter into international agreements and conduct thorough assessments of law, safety, the environment, and public acceptance.
"In Peninsular Malaysia in particular, there is insufficient baseload supply. Solar is intermittent and unpredictable; our current baseload is gas. We know we rely on imported gas, with volatile prices. We have coal, but that will no longer be usable by 2044. Our biogas and biomass are limited.
"So we need a mix. During dry seasons, when water is scarce, hydropower can't generate enough power. That is why the world is now looking at nuclear power," he said.
According to Fadillah, the Philippines and Vietnam have decided to include nuclear energy in their energy mixes.
"So Malaysia must consider nuclear power as one viable clean energy option for the future. If we adopt nuclear, once it's in place, it can guarantee supply for 10 to 15 years before we make changes. That needs a detailed study.
"We need a broader energy mix to ensure a secure energy supply that will support economic growth and the people's wellbeing. For Peninsular Malaysia, this is the approach.
Fadillah highlighted that Sabah faces a different challenge as the state relies heavily on diesel.
"Diesel is expensive now, so generation costs are higher. In Sarawak, about 70 per cent already comes from renewable energy; now they want to accelerate development in gas and solar," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Home Ministry to give input on Turap in Cabinet
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Home Ministry will provide its input on the proposed new foreign worker recruitment system in the Cabinet.
The proposed system, known as the Universal Recruitment Advanced Platform (Turap), was developed by Bestinet Sdn Bhd.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the ministry would respond accordingly once the proposal is formally presented at the Cabinet level.
"When the matter is brought to the Cabinet meeting, the ministry will provide its views at that time," he said at a press conference after witnessing the signing ceremony for the procurement of a second Multi-Purpose Mission Ship (MPMS) for the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) here today.
It was reported that the government has yet to decide on any digital foreign worker recruitment system, with the Human Resources Ministry pushing back against reports that a platform has already been selected.
The ministry
said no final approval has been made
, stressing that any decision would be guided by governance standards, national economic needs and Malaysia's international reputation.
The proposed system would be integrated with existing platforms, including the Foreign Worker Centralised Management System (FWCMS) and the National Integrated Immigration System (NIISe).
Engagements are ongoing with labour source countries and industry stakeholders, including Bangladesh, Indonesia and India, as well as employers' groups and industry bodies.
Last week, reports suggested the ministry saw no objection to a new system developed by
Bestinet Sdn Bhd
, known as the Turap, but it has not confirmed any final selection.
The system is being marketed as a solution that allows companies to hire workers directly, bypassing middlemen who often charge excessive fees.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'I'll slaughter you all tonight': Man jailed over threat to mother
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A scrap metal collector has been jailed for seven months after pleading guilty to threatening to kill his mother and possessing stolen copper wire.
Johari Ramlan, 36, entered the plea after two charges were read before magistrate Arun Noval Dass at the Yong Peng magistrate's court.
On the first charge, the father of two was accused of criminal intimidation after telling his 74-year-old mother: "Mak, tonight the whole family will be slaughtered by me."
The offence was committed at a house in Kampung Kelichap, Senggarang, about 9am on April 2. He was charged under Section 506 of the Penal Code.
On the second charge, Johari was found in possession of stolen copper wire at an abandoned house in Jalan Sungai Suluh Besar, Senggarang, at 3.20pm on April 17.
The offence falls under Section 29(1) of the Minor Offences Act 1955, which carries a maximum penalty of one year's imprisonment or a fine of up to RM1,000.
According to case facts, Johari had scolded his mother after she refused to let him use a mobile phone. He then issued the threat, causing the elderly woman to fear for her safety.
The incident was witnessed by his sister-in-law, and the victim later lodged a police report.
The prosecution was led by deputy public prosecutor Firdaus Ruslan, while the accused was represented by Tan Song Yan from the National Legal Aid Foundation.
The court sentenced him to four months' jail for criminal intimidation and three months for possession of stolen property. The sentences will run concurrently from April 16, the date of his arrest.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KL mayor goes on the ground to assess flood and landslide damage in Segambut
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kuala Lumpur Mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud visited several locations in the city hit by flash floods yesterday, including Bukit Lanjan, where a landslide also occurred.
In a series of videos posted to Segambut member of parliament Hannah Yeoh's Facebook page, the mayor—clad in yellow wellington boots—was seen inspecting the sites after floodwaters had subsided.
The affected locations in the Segambut district included Kampung Sungai Penchala, Kampung Segambut Dalam, and Jalan Seri Bintang.
Yesterday's heavy rain also triggered a mud flood in
Taman Tun Dr Ismail
(TTDI), occurring just a day after a stop-work order was issued for the Bukit Kiara Federal Park administrative zone office project.
The TTDI Fire and Rescue Station received an emergency call at 4.28pm and deployed a fire engine to the scene.
"No victims were reported, and the operation concluded at 5.27pm," the department said.
A video circulating on social media showed muddy water pooling near the construction site.
A
stop-work order
had been issued with immediate effect on Sunday following a previous mud flood at Jalan Haji Openg. Yeoh, who is also Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories), visited the affected areas with Fadlun.
She said that Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) has taken immediate action to mitigate risks to the safety of nearby residents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fahmi extends condolences to family of veteran newsman Joseph Bingkasan
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil has expressed his condolences to the family of Joseph Bingkasan, the former New Straits Times (NST) editorial head for Sabah and Sarawak, who died early today.
Fahmi said he was deeply saddened by the passing of the veteran journalist, whom he described as a significant contributor to the country's media industry, particularly in strengthening the editorial landscape in East Malaysia.
"My condolences to the entire family. May they be granted strength and resilience in facing this loss," he said in a Facebook post.
Fahmi also noted that Joseph was a recipient of the Tabung Kasih@HAWANA aid in 2023.
Joseph, 71, died at 3.56am at Queen Elizabeth II Hospital in Kota Kinabalu, believed to be from cancer.
He had a long and distinguished career in journalism, starting as a sub-editor with the Kinabalu Sabah Times in 1977 before joining New Straits Times Press (NSTP) as a Sabah correspondent.
He also served at Times of Malaysia London Ltd, the NST office in London, before being appointed editorial head of NST Sabah and Sarawak, based in Kuching. He also previously worked with Sabah's local newspaper, the Daily Express.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Pisang goreng' seller fined RM3,500 for outraging sister-in-law's modesty
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A "Pisang Goreng" seller was fined RM3,500 by the Yong Peng Magistrate's Court today after pleading guilty to outraging the modesty of his sister-in-law two months ago.
Magistrate Arun Noval Dass also ordered the 31-year-old accused to serve six months in jail if he failed to pay the fine. The accused paid the fine.
The father of 3 was charged with committing the offence against his 27-year-old sister-in-law at 2am on Feb 12.
The charge was framed under Section 354 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum 10 years' jail, a fine, whipping, or any two of these punishments upon conviction.
According to the facts of the case, the victim lodged a police report alleging she was molested while sleeping at her sister's house.
She had been visiting her sister, who is the wife of the accused. On the morning of the incident, the accused allegedly entered the room and molested her.
The accused was later arrested at a house in the district following the report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KLIA baggage handling disruption: Power supply system in good condition of day of incident, says TNB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tenaga Nasional Bhd (TNB) said its power supply system to the Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) was in good condition on April 18, when the airport's Baggage Handling System (BHS) experienced a breakdown.
TNB, in a statement, said no supply disruptions were recorded on the day of the incident.
"The electricity supply system to KLIA was in good condition on the day of the incident. No disruptions were recorded.
"TNB is ready to extend any technical cooperation to the relevant quarters to assist further investigation, as well as support any mitigation measures and subsequent improvements.
It added that consumers who wish to report any electricity supply interruptions to its 15454 hotline, via a private message on TNB Careline's Facebook page, direct message to @Tenaga_Nasional on X, or email CareLine@myTNB.my
On Saturday, the baggage handling system at KLIA's Terminal 1 experienced a breakdown, causing delays of two to four hours for many passengers.
The Transport Ministry (MOT) in a statement yesterday said it had identified the root cause of the problem and that immediate and long-term mitigation measures are being implemented to prevent recurrence and strengthen system resilience.
The ministry has mandated a comprehensive overhaul of baggage-handling protocols and infrastructure at KLIA in light of the disruption.
It will table a Cabinet Note regarding the incident tomorrow.
At the same time, the Civil Aviation Authority of Malaysia (CAAM) is currently undertaking an investigation into the issue.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Maybank to shift to new HQ at Menara Merdeka 118 from May 6
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank) says it will officially relocate its head office from Menara Maybank on Jalan Tun Perak to Menara Merdeka 118 here effective from May 6.
In a statement, the country's biggest bank said the new headquarters will occupy 33 floors and accommodate about 7,000 employees.
The office will feature a dedicated entrance to improve accessibility for employees, customers and visitors.
Several subsidiaries, including Maybank Islamic Bhd and Maybank Investment Bank Bhd, will also be relocating to the new premises in a phased and staggered manner.
Maybank said its new registered address will be Level 70, Menara Merdeka 118, Presint Merdeka 118, 50118 Kuala Lumpur.
It added that the Kuala Lumpur main branch at Menara Maybank will remain fully operational, with no disruption to customers until further notice.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Scarce food, bleak futures spur Rohingya refugees to gamble with death at sea
Article:
ROHINGYA refugee Rahila Begum spent two days adrift in the Andaman Sea this month, clinging to a wooden shard after her overcrowded boat capsized, one of the few survivors of a disaster that left 250 missing and feared dead.
She was among the thousands of Rohingya Muslims who brave hunger and accidents on rickety boats each year to flee desperate conditions in camps in southeastern Bangladesh for countries such as Indonesia and Malaysia.
Hundreds die en route from hunger or accidents at sea, but the numbers keep growing as shrinking food rations caused by dwindling international aid push yet more to make the dangerous crossing.
"I never thought I would survive," said Begum, her voice thready from fever and aches as she sat, wrapped in a blanket, on a thin mat in her parents' shack thrown together from tarpaulin sheets. "It felt like the end of my life."
The 26-year-old was rescued by a passing Bangladeshi oil tanker after her boat, with nearly 300 aboard, sank this month on its way to Malaysia, and later handed to the country's Coast Guard.
Bangladesh's coastal district of Cox's Bazar is home to nearly 1.2 million Rohingya, many of whom fled persecution and fighting in Buddhist-majority Myanmar, where they are accused of being outsiders.
Trapped for years, denied the right to work, receiving only limited education and shrinking food aid, few see a future in Bangladesh and cannot risk returning to Myanmar.
UN refugee agency UNHCR says nearly 900 Rohingya were reported missing or dead in the Andaman Sea and Bay of Bengal in 2025, making it the deadliest year on record for regional sea crossings, with more than 6,500 attempted.
Between January and mid-April this year, more than 2,800 Rohingya attempted such journeys, the agency says.
[[nid:1422859]]
"The Rohingya population is very young and aspires to a better life, but that hope is increasingly turning into desperation," said Astrid Castelein, a UNHCR official.
"That is why youths and families are deciding to take these dangerous boat journeys."
Authorities have stepped up coastal patrols and surveillance of the camps to curb trafficking networks, a Bangladesh official said, speaking on condition of anonymity, but acknowledged that the scale of desperation made enforcement difficult.
Begum's arms and body are scarred with burns caused by spills of hot engine oil mixed with seawater.
She left Myanmar in 2017 during a military crackdown that sent more than 730,000 Rohingya to Bangladesh amid accusations of killings, mass rape and arson – acts a United Nations mission later described as "genocidal", a charge Myanmar denies.
Things worsened after her marriage four years ago, she said.
"I was beaten because I could not have children. I knew I had to flee. There was no life left for me here," she said, adding that her husband had refused to take her back.
This month the UN World Food Programme began distributing food rations in Cox's Bazar on the basis of households' earning capacity.
The monthly sums handed out range from US$12 per person for the most vulnerable to US$7 for those deemed food insecure, with families headed by children, women and the elderly getting the most support.
"My ration has been cut from US$12 to US$7 because I have an 18-year-old son," said Mohammed Rafiq, 50, a father of four. "But does he earn anything?"
The money was enough to buy only rice and cooking oil but not the fish or meat his children hanker for.
"We are living in inhumane conditions," he added, fighting a lack of proper food, shelter, freedom, education and work. "Even if my children leave by sea one day, I would not be surprised."
That desperation is often exploited by traffickers, many of whom are Rohingya themselves.
[[nid:1422858]]
Among them is a 24-year-old who identified himself by his nickname, Faisal, who said he had sent 20 people, three women and two children among them, on the capsized boat, but none survived the disaster.
He avoided the telephone calls of families seeking answers, he said. "They keep calling again and again … sometimes I just switch off my phone."
Faisal said he first travelled to Malaysia in 2018 with the help of traffickers before returning to the camps to enter the trade.
Although Bangladesh jailed him in 2020 for a year in a human trafficking case, that did not stop him from taking up the trade again after his release.
Reuters could not independently confirm his account.
Such journeys usually peak during the calmer winter months, Faisal added, but many desperate to get away from the camps are now willing to take more risks.
"They come to us asking for a way out," he said. "They know the risks – some make it, some are arrested, some die."
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 23-year-old soldier faces child sexual assault charges
Article:
MELAKA: A soldier has been charged in a Sessions Court in Ayer Keroh with two counts of sexual offences against two young girls, as well as possessing obscene material on his mobile phone.
Private Muhammad Fudhail Fathollah Hamdan, 23, said he understood the charges when they were read out in court and claimed trial.
The case was heard before judge Raja Noor Adilla Raja Mahyaldin, with the prosecution led by deputy public prosecutor Muhammad Qaiyyum Ramlan and the defence represented by SS Amritpal Singh.
According to the charges, the accused is alleged to have committed sexual assault against an eight-year-old girl by pulling her into a house and pushing her against a wall.
The offence allegedly took place at an unoccupied house in Kem Terendak, Sungai Udang, about 4:30pm on April 12.
He faces a second, similar charge involving a 10-year-old girl at the same location and time.
The charges were framed under Section 14(a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017, which carries a maximum sentence of 20 years' imprisonment and whipping upon conviction.
The accused is also charged under Section 292(a) of the Penal Code for possessing obscene images and videos on his iPhone 15 Pro Max.
The alleged offence was committed at the Sexual, Women and Children Investigation Division (D11) of the Melaka police headquarters about 8pm on April 13.
Police said the victims had been playing outside when they were approached by a man they did not know, who allegedly asked them to help carry items into a house before assaulting them.
The victims later informed their families, and the suspect was arrested after being identified in the area.
Prosecutors requested bail of RM40,000 with one surety, along with additional conditions, including not contacting the victims or witnesses and reporting to a police station monthly.
The defence sought a lower amount, citing the accused's financial constraints and upcoming marriage.
The court granted bail at RM8,000 with one surety and imposed the additional conditions requested by the prosecution.
The case has been fixed for mention on May 26 for the submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Even the smallest groups must benefit, says PM Anwar
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today said even the smallest groups must benefit from the RM10 billion allocation under the Syarikat Jaminan Pembiayaan Perniagaan (SJPP).
He said the government-backed guarantee schemes should also cover agricultural groups, smallholders, fishermen, as well as small traders operating stalls and small restaurants.
"We have asked all related facilities, including CGC (Credit Guarantee Corporation Malaysia Bhd) and others, under the plans of Bank Negara Malaysia and the Treasury Department, to also involve agricultural groups, smallholders, fishermen and small traders operating stalls and small restaurants.
"We want to ensure that low-level entrepreneurs and small businesses, involving many citizens, do not feel marginalised or neglected.
"I want these announced measures to ensure that even the smallest groups benefit," he told a press conference after chairing a roundtable session with financial institutions today.
Present were Treasury secretary-general Datuk Johan Mahmood Merican and Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) governor Datuk Seri Abdul Rasheed Ghaffour.
Meanwhile, Anwar said several matters have been raised, including those related to SJPP, which require the government's attention.
"I will personally bring them to the cabinet meeting so that the approval process can be expedited and does not become overly time-consuming or bureaucratic.
"In the current situation, where we are facing various issues and crises, efficiency, decisiveness and swift decision-making are urgently needed," he said.
Earlier, Anwar announced that the government will add another
RM5 billion to support micro, small and medium-sized enterprises
(MSMEs) affected by the global energy crisis.
This brings the total allocation to support MSMEs affected by the Middle East conflict to RM10 billion.
The additional support comes after the Finance Ministry announced a series of measures to help MSMEs, which include a
RM5 billion
allocation under the SJPP yesterday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 5 held over spate of robberies
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested five men in connection with a spate of robberies in Kampung Sri Tambun, Durian Tunggal here.
Melaka police chief Datuk Dzulkhairi Mukhtar said the suspects, in  their 20s and 30s, were detained in Perak on Sunday.
"They have been remanded to facilitate investigations."
The arrests follow an armed robbery in Kampung Sri Tambun on April 12, where the robbers assaulted their victims.
At least four other families in the area said they had also been victims of the gang, whose members were masked and armed with parang. The modus operandi in all the cases, which dated back to November, was reportedly the same.
The robbers would first cut through a fence before breaking into the victims' homes through the back doors.
They struck late at night while the victims were asleep.
Following the April 12 incident, the victims submitted a memorandum to Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail and Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail, urging immediate intervention.
"We are living in fear as these incidents are not isolated. Many homes have been targeted, yet there is no clear resolution," it said.
Residents also raised concerns over the gang's increasingly sophisticated methods, including the alleged use of signal jammers.
"This is no longer an ordinary crime. The use of technology suggests the involvement of a well-organised group."
The robberies have renewed fears of a resurgence of the so-called Durian Tunggal gang, whose alleged members were shot dead by police in November last year.
Authorities had linked the group to at least 20 cases in Melaka, as well as incidents in Negri Sembilan and Selangor, with total losses estimated at RM1.35 million.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SMK Taman Selesa Jaya students bag four golds at Terengganu science fair
Article:
JOHOR BARU: SMK Taman Selesa Jaya students clinched four gold medals and the best presenter award at the Terengganu International Science Fair 2026.
The three-day competition, held at Universiti Malaysia Terengganu from April 16 to 18, drew participants from across the region, showcasing cutting-edge student research and innovation.
The fair is a science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM) competition, where students present and defend research and innovation projects before expert judges.
It provides a platform for students to develop real-world solutions, while exposing them to a conference-style scientific environment.
The Johor school emerged as one of the standout performers in the applied science, environmental science and artificial intelligence (AI) categories, clinching four gold medals and the prestigious best presenter award.
SMK Taman Selesa Jaya's M. Kirthika won a gold medal and was named best presenter.
The 15-year-old's well-articulated environmental science project presentation was clear and delivered with confidence, impressing the judges.
Also securing gold medals in team presentations were siblings G. Sarrveendranath, 14, and G. Harrveendranath, 10, in the applied science category, while P. Varnika, 13, secured another gold in the same category.
Kirthika's younger brother, M. Jayesh Ram, 13, bagged the school's fourth gold medal in the AI category.
Behind the students' success was the guidance of their teacher, M. Pherarasi, whose mentorship was instrumental in shaping a culture of inquiry and innovation within the school.
"The students' research presentations stood out for their originality and practical relevance, which I believe contributed to the gold medal haul," she said.
She said that her students were disciplined and consistent, spending months preparing their presentations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Housewife arrested over seizure of five guns, claims weapons belong to husband
Article:
KOTA BARU: The housewife who was arrested after police seized five firearms, including an M16 rifle, from a house in Kampung Miak yesterday, has claimed the weapons belong to her husband.
General Operations Force (GOF) Southeast Brigade commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain said the woman claimed she did not know about the firearms.
Checks revealed her 34-year-old husband has no prior criminal record.
"Among the items seized were a Colt M16 A1 rifle, two shotguns, including a short gun, two homemade air rifles, and an air pump.
"Also found were 57 shotgun pellets, 20 rounds of 5.56 mm ammunition, another 20 rounds of 5.56 mm ammunition, and 35 live rounds," he said in a statement today.
Ahmad Radzi said the woman told investigators she did not know where her husband acquired the weapons.
"She added that she had only moved back into the house four months ago after previously living with her family in Kuala Krai.
"She reportedly attempted to contact her husband several times to ask him to return, but he refused."
The suspect and the
seized items,
valued at RM24,875, have been handed over to the Gua Musang District Criminal Investigation Department.
The was remanded for five days starting today. The case is being investigated under Section 8 of the Firearms Act 1971.
The discovery was made at 6.45pm yesterday during a raid by a team from the GOF 8th Battalion under Ops Bersepadu Khazanah.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia not passive on sovereignty despite neutral diplomatic stance - Fadillah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia will not remain passive on issues of international sovereignty despite its neutral diplomatic stance, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said.
He described the country's diplomacy as pragmatic and independent, with Malaysia maintaining good relations with all parties while upholding principles of global justice.
"When we say we are neutral, it does not mean we are passive or unclear in our position. We have principles, and we engage pragmatically with all relevant parties," he said when featured on Bernama Radio's exclusive interview on how geopolitical crisis drives Malaysia's energy transition, at Wisma Bernama here today.
Fadillah was met on arrival at the Malaysian National News Agency's (Bernama) headquarters by its chairman, Datuk Seri Wong Chun Wai, and chief executive officer Datin Paduka Nur-ul Afida Kamaludin.
Also present were editor-in-chief Arul Rajoo Durar Raj, deputy editor-in-chief (News Services) Mohd Shukri Ishak and acting deputy editor-in-chief (Economic News Services) Harlina Samson.
Fadillah said Malaysia is also actively engaged in diplomatic efforts with leaders from countries such as Turkiye and Pakistan, while working with the United Nations (UN) and the United States to seek peaceful solutions.
Despite taking firm positions on sovereignty, Fadillah said Malaysia continues to balance its diplomatic ties, particularly in trade, to safeguard economic growth.
"Our trade relations with China, the US, Europe and the Asean region continue to grow," he said, adding that Malaysia's overarching diplomatic goal is to promote global stability to support sustainable economic growth and shared prosperity.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hilux driver probed over dangerous Cameron Highlands overtake
Article:
IPOH: Police have identified the driver seen repeatedly attempting a dangerous overtaking manoeuvre along the winding stretch between Simpang Pulai and Cameron Highlands.
District police chief Muhammad Najib Hamzah said the 66-year-old man from Kampung Raja, Cameron Highlands, will have his statement recorded over the incident.
"No official reports have been received, but the owner of the vehicle has been identified.
"We will call him to have his statement recorded," he said when contacted.
Najib said the timing of the incident has yet to be determined, as investigations are ongoing.
"I can't verify when it happened. The owner has yet to meet the investigating officer," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 42 of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless driving, which carries a penalty of up to five years' imprisonment, a fine of between RM5,000 and RM15,000, and disqualification from holding a driving licence for at least five years upon conviction.
A video circulating on social media
has drawn criticism after the driver of a silver Toyota Hilux was seen repeatedly attempting to overtake along the same winding stretch.
The clip, shared by the Malaysian Road Vigilante Facebook page, shows the four-wheel drive tailing closely behind two other 4WD vehicles and a white car believed to be slowing traffic on the narrow, single-carriageway road marked with double lines.
Despite continuous bends and limited visibility, the driver crossed the double lines multiple times in an apparent attempt to overtake, risking a head-on collision with oncoming traffic.
The attempts, which lasted more than two minutes, were unsuccessful as the driver repeatedly pulled back into the lane due to approaching vehicles.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sultan Nazrin: Planetary health must be the central pillar of Asean security
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Planetary health must be the central pillar of Asean security as ecological breakdown is already displacing populations, disrupting livelihoods and undermining regional stability.
Sultan of Perak Sultan Nazrin Muizzuddin Shah said ecological breakdown is one of the most under-acknowledged security threats of the era, even though its effects are already visible across the region.
"It is one that does not manifest through missiles or cyberattacks, but is already displacing populations, destroying harvests, collapsing fisheries and redrawing coastlines across our region," he said in his keynote address at the Putrajaya Forum.
Sultan Nazrin said climate change, biodiversity loss, ocean acidification, water scarcity and soil degradation must be treated as core security threats, rather than issues separate from the security agenda.
"These are not environmental issues that sit alongside our security agenda. They are security threats that belong at its centre," he said.
He said these pressures are already generating displacement, resource competition, food insecurity and state fragility, with several Asean member states already dealing with their consequences.
Sultan Nazrin cautioned that treating planetary health separately from security policy is a strategic misjudgement.
This, he said, as environmental degradation strikes at the foundation of national and regional stability, regardless of technological or military advancement.
"To treat planetary health as a separate conversation from security is not merely an analytical error; it is a strategic one.
"A region that cannot feed itself, water itself or protect its coastlines from inundation cannot be secure in any meaningful sense, however sophisticated its military technologies are," he said.
Sultan Nazrin said environmental security must be viewed as the foundation of comprehensive security, not a secondary or deferred concern.
"Environmental security is not a luxury agenda to be deferred until wealthier times. It is the foundation on which every other aspect of planetary and human security rests," he said.
As such, Sultan Nazrin urged Asean to formally adopt planetary health as a core pillar of its security framework, grounded in strategic necessity rather than symbolism.
"I would therefore urge this Forum to adopt planetary health as a formal pillar of Asean's comprehensive security framework, not as a gesture of environmental conscience, but as a recognition of strategic reality," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Saifuddin: No impulse buys in Home Ministry procurements
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Home Ministry will use the Defence Services Asia (DSA) and National Security Asia (NATSEC) 2026 exhibition as a platform to evaluate new technologies and assets to strengthen enforcement agencies under its purview.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the exhibition brings together global industry players showcasing advancements in intelligence systems, radar capabilities and other security technologies.
"DSA is a valuable opportunity where companies present their latest products in one place, compared with having to source them individually from different countries," he told reporters after witnessing the contract signing ceremony for the acquisition of a second Multi Purpose Mission Ship (MPMS) for the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) here today.
However, Saifuddin said any procurement decisions would not be made on impulse but would be guided by the ministry's long-term strategic planning.
"The ministry does not operate in a knee-jerk or impromptu manner.
"We already have a structured long-term policy framework in place to fulfil our core responsibility of maintaining public order and national security," he said.
He said ministry officials attending the exhibition would evaluate products based on identified operational needs and capability gaps.
"This is an opportunity for our officers to observe, assess and match available technologies with the requirements outlined in our long-term plans," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI must strengthen social cohesion, not divide societies, says Sultan Nazrin
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems must be designed to reinforce social cohesion rather than erode it, as poorly governed technologies risk deepening divisions and accelerating conditions that could lead to conflict.
The Sultan of Perak, Sultan Nazrin Muizzuddin Shah, said Asean must ensure that artificial intelligence is deliberately shaped to support unity and trust within societies.
"This is not merely a philosophical issue but a security concern, as AI systems optimised for engagement, profit or ideological reinforcement risk fracturing trust and amplifying grievances.
"An AI that divides is, in a meaningful sense, a weapon, even if no one intended it as such. The question of what values are encoded in these systems, and whose values they represent, is therefore a matter of statecraft," he said in his royal keynote address at the Putrajaya Forum.
Sultan Nazrin said AI development is currently concentrated in a small number of corporations and states, with incentives prioritising scale and market dominance over social cohesion and cultural diversity.
For Asean, he said this presents a pressing challenge given the region's linguistic and cultural diversity, warning that systems trained on external assumptions may not serve Southeast Asian societies effectively.
"Asean must insist on prosocial by design AI systems built on equity and inclusion, supported by transparency, accountability and independent auditing.
"It is the condition under which innovation earns its legitimacy. The question is not whether Asean will use AI. We will, and we already do.
"The question is whether we will be architects of its application in our region or merely its consumers," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MTT Shipping dismisses Melaka Straits disruption fears, cites Hormuz contrast
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: MTT Shipping & Logistics Bhd (MTTSL) has dismissed concerns over potential disruptions to the Strait of Melaka, drawing a clear distinction between the regional waterway and the conflict-affected Strait of Hormuz.
Speaking at the press conference of its listing ceremony here today, MTT managing director and Shipping Association of Malaysia chairman Ooi Lean Hin said the risk profiles of the two strategic maritime routes differ significantly, particularly in terms of accessibility and global trade implications.
"In the case of the Strait of Hormuz, it is effectively a dead end. When it is closed, vessels have no alternative route and are forced to remain in place," he said.
By contrast, Ooi emphasised that even in a hypothetical scenario where the Straits of Melaka faces disruption, which he noted is highly unlikely, shipping routes could still be rerouted through alternative passages.
The remarks come amid heightened global attention on maritime chokepoints following ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, which have disrupted vessel movement in the Strait of Hormuz and driven up freight rates due to constrained capacity.
As an international waterway bordered by Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore, the Straits of Melaka is subjected to shared responsibility among littoral states such as Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore to ensure it remains open and navigable.
"They can have their eyes on the Straits of Melaka, but they cannot do much in terms of closing it," added Ooi, referring to the US' current interest in the route.
He added that unlike the Gulf region, where vessels remain stranded due to restricted passage, Southeast Asia has not experienced similar large-scale disruptions, with shipping activity continuing largely as normal.
Ooi said the company remains focused on long-term growth driven by intra-Asia trade, particularly between China, Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Policeman reassigned after viral footage shows him stepping on suspect's head
Article:
KULIM: A policeman caught on camera stepping on a suspect's head during an arrest yesterday has been reassigned to administrative duties pending an internal investigation.
Kulim deputy police chief Deputy Superintendent Tengku Mohd Faisal Tengku Yeng said the Integrity and Standard Compliance Department (JIPS) has been directed to probe the incident and determine if standard operating procedures were breached.
Meanwhile, the 32-year-old suspect who was seen being pinned down in the viral video has been remanded for three days starting today to assist in investigations.
The
incident
occurred in front of a motorcycle accessory shop on Jalan Tunku Putra during a crime prevention patrol.
A 29-second video circulating on Facebook shows the suspect being pulled from his motorcycle. After falling to the ground, a policeman is seen stepping on his head.
Police said a struggle ensued during the arrest after the suspect allegedly kicked a police motorcycle and attempted to flee by riding recklessly against the flow of traffic.
Following his arrest, police seized two plastic bags suspected of containing boiled ketum juice. The suspect also tested positive for methamphetamine.
The case is being investigated under Section 30(3) of the Poisons Act 1952, Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, and Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King presents instruments of appointment to 14 judges
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, today granted an audience and presented the instruments of appointment to 14 judges at Istana Negara, here.
The ceremony began with the presentation of the instruments of appointment as Federal Court Judges to Datuk Ravinthran Paramaguru and Datuk Azmi Ariffin.
[[nid:1422832]]
His Majesty also presented the instruments of appointment to six Court of Appeal judges.
They are Datuk Amarjeet Singh Serjit Singh, Datuk Seri Mohd Radzi Harun, Datuk Aliza Sulaiman, Datuk Ahmad Shahrir Mohd Salleh, Dean Wayne Daly and Datuk Seri Latifah Mohd Tahar.
Six High Court judges also received their instruments of appointment from the King today.
They are Dr Suzana Muhamad Said, Yusrin Faidz Yusoff, Arziah Mohamed Apandi, Datuk Hazizah Kassim, Gan Techiong and Suriyati Hasimah Mohd Hashim.
The ceremony was also attended by Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said, and Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar.
Also present were Chief Justice Datuk Seri Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh and the Keeper of the Rulers' Seal Tan Sri Syed Danial Syed Ahmad.
At a separate ceremony, Sultan Ibrahim granted an audience and presented the instruments of appointment to 17 Penang Islamic Religious Council (MAINPP) members.
They included MAINPP president Datuk Dr Mohamad Abdul Hamid, its deputy president Datuk Ahmad Shaziy Ismail Bakti, State Secretary Datuk Seri Zulkifli Long and State Legal Adviser Datuk Wan Nor Sakina Saad.
Also receiving the instruments of appointment were State Financial Officer Datuk Zairi Mat Ali, Penang Mufti Datuk Dr Mohd Sukki Othman, Penang Police Chief Datuk Azizee Ismail and Istana Negara Religious Officer Datuk Munir Md Salleh.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zambry: Tun Razak legacy still shapes Malaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tun Abdul Razak Hussein's legacy endures and remains central to Malaysia's policies and institutions, said Higher Education Minister Datuk Seri Dr Zambry Abdul Kadir.
Speaking at the 'Tun Abdul Razak: Honouring the Legacy and Inspiring the Future' forum at Universiti Malaya, Zambry said the former prime minister's leadership was defined by an "others-centred" approach rather than self-glorification.
Zambry said academia often views legacy through personality and monuments, or 'Legacy From', while Tun Razak embodied 'The Legacy For', with a focus on shaping a nation for future generations.
The minister shared a memory of being a 14-year-old boy in Pulau Pangkor when the statesman died in 1976.
He recalled how news of the death reached the remote fishing community via a shared Utusan Melayu Jawi newspaper at a local coffee shop and the village's only television set.
"I still remember the tears in Pulau Pangkor. Tun Razak had personally visited to see the suffering and poverty of fishermen. His passing was deeply felt because he was a leader who truly walked among the people," said Zambry, himself the son of a fisherman.
Zambry highlighted Tun Razak's "Nation Builder" legacy through three pillars: Adaptive, Transformative and Consensus Building.
Tun Razak was the country's second prime minister, serving from 1970 to 1976. He is fondly remembered as the father of Malaysia's development policies and rural transformation.
Zambry, who is also Barisan Nasional secretary-general, said the principle of political consultation introduced by Tun Razak remains the foundation of the coalition's constitution today.
"He did not choose the easy path, introducing a brave, principled approach to social justice and systematic rural development. He ensured the country moved strategically, not reactively," he said.
He also highlighted the 1956 Razak Report as a "civilisational project" that redefined national identity through education.
By making education the main route out of poverty, Tun Razak opened doors for children of labourers, farmers and fishermen.
"If we speak of social mobility today, we are standing on the foundation he built," he said, urging young people to study founding leaders for direction in "post-normal times."
The forum, marking the 50th anniversary of Tun Razak's passing, was attended by UM Board chairman Tan Sri Zarinah Anwar, Tun Razak's son Tan Sri Nazir Razak, and other family members.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Home Ministry to verify documents of Kampung Bahagia fire victims
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Home Ministry will assess the documentation status of victims of the Kampung Bahagia fire in Sandakan on a case-by-case basis, as authorities continue registration efforts following the disaster.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said more than 9,000 people were affected, with over 1,000 houses in the water village destroyed.
He said Sabah authorities, together with federal agencies, are carrying out a registration exercise, with priority given to providing temporary shelter for displaced residents.
"Our immediate focus is to ensure victims are placed in temporary evacuation centres. Registration is ongoing, and only after that will we be able to determine documentation status," he told reporters after witnessing the signing of a contract for the
acquisition of a second Multi-Purpose Mission Ship (MPMS)
for the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency.
Saifuddin said cases involving individuals without valid identification documents would be handled carefully.
"For genuine Malaysian citizens who lost their documents due to the fire, we will facilitate replacements. However, this does not mean all victims automatically qualify. Each case will be assessed individually," he said.
He stressed that identity documentation is a critical matter and cannot be issued indiscriminately.
"We must ensure that documents are only issued to those who are entitled. At the same time, we will assist citizens affected by the incident who require replacements," he said.
On April 19,
a pre-dawn fire destroyed homes
in Kampung Bahagia, Sandakan. The Sabah Fire and Rescue Department received a distress call at 1.32am and arrived at the scene 16 minutes later after travelling about 15km.
The blaze engulfed non-permanent houses built on water, spreading rapidly due to narrow access routes, low tide, strong winds and the close proximity of homes, which also hampered firefighting efforts.
Personnel from the Kinabatangan station assisted in the operation, with water sourced from tankers and nearby hydrants.
Subsequent reports indicated the scale of the disaster had widened, with about 1,000 of the area's 1,200 stilt houses affected,
involving 9,007 residents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Probe into SOP after policeman filmed stepping on suspect during Kulim arrest
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Authorities have launched an investigation to determine if policemen adhered to standard operating procedures (SOP) during an arrest in Kulim, Kedah, where one policeman was filmed stepping on a suspect.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said he has instructed the Kedah police chief to expedite investigations into the incident.
"All enforcement actions by police are governed by established SOPs.
"We need to determine whether there was compliance or a breach," he said after witnessing the contract signing for the acquisition of a second Multi Purpose Mission Ship (MPMS) for the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) today.
Saifuddin said further action, including a review by the police integrity unit, would depend on the outcome of the investigation.
"If necessary, the Integrity and Standards Compliance Department will be engaged to assess whether there was any violation. But we must first examine all aspects thoroughly," he said.
He cautioned against making premature judgments based solely on brief video clips circulating online.
"What we see is only a short duration of the incident.
We must also consider what happened before that. Was the suspect aggressive? Did he ride against traffic? Was there any attempt to ram police vehicles? These factors must be investigated," he said.
Saifuddin stressed that a balanced approach was necessary to avoid undermining police morale while ensuring accountability.
"We cannot act rashly, as it could affect the morale of officers carrying out enforcement duties, especially when dealing with potentially aggressive suspects," he said.
It was
reported earlier
that police had confirmed that the man seen in the viral video had earlier resisted arrest and later tested positive for drugs.
The incident on Jalan Tunku Putra occurred during a crime prevention patrol operation.
A 29-second video circulating on Facebook shows a policeman pulling a man off a motorcycle before stepping on his head.
The incident took place at 11am while police personnel were conducting a patrol and attempting to detain a suspect on Jalan Sungai Ular.
During the attempted arrest, a struggle ensued as the suspect resisted by kicking a policeman's motorcycle and fleeing.
However, police managed to track the suspect to a motorcycle accessories shop on Jalan Tunku Putra and detained him shortly after.
A search of the motorcycle ridden by the 32-year-old suspect led to the discovery of two plastic bags believed to contain ketum juice.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt adds RM5bil support for MSMEs amid global energy crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government will add another RM5 billion to support micro, small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) affected by the global energy crisis, said Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
This brings the total allocation to support MSMEs affected by the Middle East conflict to RM10 billion.
The additional support comes after the Finance Ministry announced a series of measures to help MSMEs, which include a RM5 billion allocation under the Syarikat Jaminan Pembiayaan Perniagaan (SJPP) on Monday.
"We have added another RM5 billion under SJPP to assist affected sectors, especially SMEs in logistics, construction and farming.
"I have requested from Bank Negara Malaysia for the additional amount, and the governor will announce further details on the allocation soon," he said in a press briefing following a roundtable meeting with chief executive officers of financial institutions earlier today.
Anwar said that the financing support should include smaller entrepreneurs to ensure none of them is sidelined from government assistance amid the uncertain operating environment.
"We need to include those operating at a small scale in this initiative, such as fishermen, stall operators and smallholders. I want this financing support to benefit them.
"I will bring this matter to the Cabinet meeting to expedite the process and reduce bureaucracy on the matter because of the desperate times we are in that require immediate action," said Anwar.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geopolitical tensions slash Malaysia’s West Asia trade by 30.4pc, says Matrade chief
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — Malaysia’s trade with West Asia fell 30.4 per cent to RM21.41 billion amid ongoing geopolitical tensions, said Datuk Seri Reezal Merican Naina Merican.
The chairman of the Malaysia External Trade Development Corporation (Matrade) said exports to the region dropped 23.6 per cent to RM9.87 billion, mainly due to lower shipments of palm oil, petroleum products and electrical and electronic (E&E) goods.
“The conflict has disrupted supply chains and pushed up costs, including freight charges and insurance, adding pressure on exporters.
“While the direct impact is still limited, we are monitoring the situation closely due to potential spillover effects on global trade,” Reezal Merican told a press briefing at the Matrade headquarters here today.
MORE TO COME

Title: Flooded stretches on Jalan Ampang Hilir, Jalan Kuching snarl KL traffic
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — Several major roads in the city centre were hit by flash floods today after continuous rain since morning, disrupting traffic flow.
In a Facebook post, the Kuala Lumpur Control Centre (KLCCC) under Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) said the persistent rainfall had caused water to accumulate at multiple locations.
Among the affected areas was Jalan Ampang Hilir, particularly the stretch in front of the Saudi Arabian Embassy.
Vehicles drive through floodwaters along Jalan Kuching in Kuala Lumpur after heavy rain. — Picture via Facebook/dbklklccc
Flash floods were also reported along Jalan Kuching near the World Trade Centre Kuala Lumpur (WTC), heading towards the Segambut roundabout.
“Traffic police personnel and traffic wardens are on site to manage the situation,” the post said.
Motorists have been advised to exercise caution and plan their journeys to avoid affected routes.

Title: Court orders additional 10 months’ jail for 33 men who stormed Taman Shamelin KTV after previously imposing fines
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — A High Court decision has extended the sentences of 33 men involved in an attack on a KTV outlet in Taman Shamelin, ordering them to serve an additional 10 months in prison.
According to
The Star,
Justice Datuk Aslam Zainuddin delivered the ruling today while reviewing a prosecution appeal against a Magistrate's Court judgment from earlier in the month.
The lower court had previously imposed fines ranging from RM2,000 to RM3,000 on the men. Justice Aslam ordered that the new jail term run from the date of sentencing.
According to earlier proceedings on April 10, 32 of the accused were fined RM3,000 each — with a default sentence of four months — while two 19-year-olds were fined RM2,000 each, or three months in default. All had pleaded guilty to rioting while armed with weapons, ammunition, explosives, sticks and stones capable of being used in an assault.
The incident took place around 7.30pm on April 5 at an entertainment premises in Cheras. The charge, under Section 148 of the Penal Code, carries a maximum penalty of five years' imprisonment, a fine, or both upon conviction.
The 33 convicted individuals range in age from 21 to 57. Their names are Tan Chee Chin, G. Bathmanathan, M. Kogilan, Lim Puay Fei, Kevin Yong Kai Wen, Ong Zhi Jian, Looi Khai Xuan, Voison Chor, Tan Jun Ning, Hee Tuck Choy, Ching Thong Hai, Kee Jin Xuan, Lim Teong Yow, Chan Kuang Man, Ho Chee Seong, Wong Chee Siong, Liang Chung Weng, G. Srikanth, Lee Ming Choong, Wong Wai Khuen, Khor Ka Lok, Ong Zheng Yuan, Yam Hao Jie, Lim Jia Cheng, Chai Chun You, Kher Kuan Heng, Ong Aik Chim, Chong Khai Jun, Hadzrin Julasrin, Lee Tuck Wah and Chin Chin Chung. The two young offenders are Alvin Yong Kai Zhe and Chong Yu Heng.
All had already paid their fines. A warrant of arrest was issued for another individual, A. Ganesan, who had not paid the fine and did not appear in court today.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Datuk Mohd Nordin Ismail argued before the High Court that the Magistrate’s decision to impose only fines had drawn public criticism. He said the group had carried out the attack in large numbers and that a mere fine did not properly reflect the seriousness of the offence.
Defence lawyer Damien Chan submitted that no one was injured during the incident. "They only damaged property and had pleaded guilty at the first instance. They are truly remorseful over what happened," he told the court.
According to the facts of the case, police received a report on April 5 stating that a lorry had been driven into the front gate of the entertainment outlet. A group of men then entered the premises and caused damage inside the compound. Investigators found that the suspects were armed during the attack.
A one-minute video of the incident, which later went viral, showed a lorry crashing through the main gate of the KTV outlet, followed by the group entering through the breach while carrying various objects.

Title: Matrade chairman: Malaysia’s trade shrugs off global jitters, posts 9.3pc growth in March
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — Malaysia’s trade grew 9.3 per cent year-on-year to RM272.95 billion in March 2026, driven by strong demand for electrical and electronic (E&E) goods.
Malaysia External Trade Development Corporation (Matrade) chairman Datuk Seri Reezal Merican Naina Merican said exports rose 8.3 per cent to RM148.75 billion, alongside gains in petroleum products and optical equipment.
“For the first quarter, exports expanded 12.7 per cent to RM426.53 billion, pushing the trade surplus up 54 per cent to RM63.22 billion.
“Malaysia’s trade performance remains resilient despite global uncertainty, supported by strong demand and a diversified market base,” Reezal Merican told a press briefing at the Matrade headquarters here today.
MORE TO COME

Title: Cemetery couple freed after Penang High Court revises jail term to time served following accepted representation
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — The Penang High Court in George Town has revised a 12-month custodial sentence imposed on a couple convicted of committing an indecent act at a Chinese cemetery, cutting the term to time already served and ordering their immediate release, in a ruling that underscores the role of negotiated representations in sentencing outcomes.
In allowing the criminal revision, judge Rofiah Mohamad maintained the conviction against M. Jegathesan, 58, and Halila Abu Bakar, 37, but replaced the original sentence with imprisonment from the date of arrest on March 23 until the disposal of the revision today.
“Based on the developments as informed to the court earlier, the conviction against both accused is maintained.
“However, the 12-month sentence is replaced with a term of imprisonment from the date of arrest until today. This revision is allowed,” she said, as reported by the
New Straits Times
.
Rofiah said there was no reason to depart from the representation agreed upon by both the prosecution and the defence, effectively endorsing a negotiated position that focused solely on sentencing rather than conviction.
The couple had earlier pleaded guilty before a magistrate’s court on March 26 to a charge under Section 377D of the Penal Code, receiving a one-year jail term.
Deputy public prosecutor Muna Mohamed Jaafar told the High Court that while the defence’s appeal remained at the case management stage, a separate criminal revision application had been filed following a representation submitted by the defence and accepted by the prosecution.
She said the agreement maintained the conviction but sought a reduction in the custodial term.
“The conviction is maintained… as for the sentence, the detention and imprisonment can be reduced,” she said.
Defence counsel Datuk Naran Singh said his clients had withdrawn their appeal to proceed with the revision, arguing that the sentence imposed was disproportionate.
“The maximum jail sentence is two years. The one-year imprisonment imposed on both applicants is too excessive.
“We proposed that the 12-month jail term be replaced with imprisonment from the date of arrest on March 23, until the disposal of the revision today,” he said.
Outside court, the couple expressed relief and thanked their lawyer, who had represented them pro bono.
Naran said the decision meant they had served only 28 days in custody.
“I’m very happy about this revision. They were sentenced to 12 months, but today they only got 28 days. That is the minimum sentence,” he said.
“We did not disturb the conviction. The conviction stays, but the sentence is set aside because it was too excessive for this particular case,” he added.

Title: Perak struggles to attract major hotels as tourists skip overnight stays
Article:
IPOH, April 21 — Difficulties in attracting major international hotel brands to Perak were highlighted in the State Legislative Assembly, as most tourists visiting the state prefer day trips rather than overnight stays.
Perak Tourism, Industry, Investment and Corridor Development Committee chairman Loh Sze Yee said the state continues to be seen largely as a day-trip destination among travellers.
“Whether travelling from north to south or vice versa, many visitors stop in Perak for day trips, where they eat, drink and visit places such as Teluk Intan, Manjung, Perak Tengah and Taiping.
“However, after spending the day here, they usually continue their journey and stay overnight in Penang. Likewise, visitors from Penang often come to Perak for the day but choose to stay overnight in Kuala Lumpur instead,” he said.
Loh was responding to a supplementary question from Simpang Pulai assemblyman Wong Chai Yi who had asked about the state government’s strategies to encourage visitors to extend their stay and promote repeat visits to Perak.
Loh pointed out that current travel trends are making it challenging for Perak to draw in major international hotel brands.
“These brands typically look for strong overnight stay numbers before deciding to invest,” he said.
Without solid figures showing tourists choosing to stay overnight in places like Ipoh and Taiping, Loh said, it becomes difficult to persuade global hotel players to set up here.
To tackle this, Loh said the state government is working closely with local councils and tourism agencies to improve the situation and boost visitor retention.
“One of the key strategies is to expand the range of quality accommodation, especially boutique hotels that align with today’s travel preferences,” he said.
He also noted that not every traveller is looking for luxury as many consider a clean, comfortable space with a good bed and a pleasant atmosphere more than enough.
Boutique hotels, he explained, fit this demand well since most tourists spend their time exploring rather than staying indoors.
“Travellers today tend to head out early, eager to discover the city. Some even skip hotel breakfasts, opting instead to hunt for local favourites or trending food spots.
“In Ipoh, developments like D’Kelly Hotel reflect this shift, offering themed stays that appeal to visitors looking for something different,” he said.
Loh also mentioned MU PetiTE Hotel, which provides compact yet functional rooms tailored for families.
“While the rooms may be smaller, they are designed to accommodate both parents and children comfortably in a single space, reducing the need to book multiple rooms,” he said.
At the same time, Loh said the state is not neglecting larger-scale developments, with several branded hotels already in progress.
He noted that AC Hotel by Marriott Ipoh has already started operations, while Wyndham Hotel Ipoh is currently being built.
Another project, M Hill, is also expected to open in the future.
These developments, Loh said, signal that Perak is steadily strengthening its hospitality landscape.

Title: Syed Saddiq warns new prosecutor role could be among Malaysia’s most powerful, calls for checks
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — The proposed law to separate the role of public prosecutor (PP) from the attorney general (AG) could result in the former being one of the most powerful persons in Malaysia, according to Muar MP Syed Saddiq Abdul Rahman.
For this reason alone is why he said the power to appoint the PP should not be decided by one person, but decentralised during the selection and appointment process.
“I just want to draw a picture of what I predict politics will be like if it goes wrong. Effectively if this law is passed, the prosecutor will be, to me, in the top five most powerful people in Malaysia.
“But the point I’m trying to make is, the role will be remarkably powerful, so no one should have complete control over that appointment process,” he said in a forum by the Malaysian Bar here yesterday.
He cautioned that the AG-PP split could risk becoming a “fake reform” where those in power would have “political cover” to go after their political opponents, if safeguards are not put into place.
“In the end, as long as there’s decentralisation of power and sufficient checks at every single level, I think that will lead to a comparatively better outcome than what we already have,” he said.
Syed Saddiq – one of the 12 members of the parliamentary special select committee (PSSC) studying ways to improve the government’s proposed Bill for the AG-PP split – outlined the problems with the existing situation in Malaysia.
He said the current system centralises power in the hands of a few, noting that the prime minister has a role or perceived indirect influence over the appointment of the head of investigative bodies, such as the Inspector-General of Police and the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission chief, and perceived influence over judges’ appointments via the Judicial Appointments Commission’s recommendations.
Constitutionally, the Yang di-Pertuan Agong appoints the AG on the prime minister’s advice.
But Syed Saddiq said that the AG currently comes under the Prime Minister’s Office.
Citing his own personal experience and observations, Syed Saddiq said such a system has allegedly resulted in cases where political allies see their charges dropped and political opponents face trumped up charges.
“And that system is problematic because it grants too much power to the hands of the few, in which power then becomes a bargaining tool or bargaining chip to strengthen the executive at the expense of the people, at the expense of transparency and the democratic process. I think that is truly problematic,” he said.
“I start to realise you cannot just blame the prime minister. It’s the system which is the main problem,” he said, noting that whoever becomes the prime minister could end up using the system to strengthen their position politically no matter how “clean” they are when they rise to the top.
Muar Member of Parliament Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman speaks at a forum discussing the separation of powers between the Attorney General and Public Prosecutor in Kuala Lumpur. April 20, 2026—Picture by Raymond Manuel
Syed Saddiq said that is why decentralising of power would be the best approach, as it could also prevent any perception of political influence over prosecution and even help address hypothetical scenarios where those involved in actual corruption cases claim they face “unfair political persecution”.
“Then so to me, it still leads to one common conclusion which is decentralised power, disperse it so that there are checks and balances, so that at that point in time, no prime minister can call the person up, do this, drop this. And even if there is the perception of it, we can remove it completely because now the process is so transparent,” he said of the potential outcome of an ideal improved version of the AG-PP split Bill.
If the most ideal version of the AG-PP split Bill does not end up materialising, Syed Saddiq said it should not be watered down to a point where prosecutorial power is just transferred from one position to another without checks and balances.
Syed Saddiq said he observed strong pushback from some of those who attended the PSSC’s latest meeting last week – including the Attorney General’s Chambers and some political quarters – on recommendations for how the Bill should look like, and shared his concern that it may make it harder to get two-third support from MPs for the Bill.
But he said he is still hopeful that the Bill could be passed, if lawmakers focus on reaching a “middle ground” or common grounds, including decentralising power such as by having more members and more diversity in the panel that recommends candidates for the PP role.
Syed Saddiq said this would ensure that the potential candidates recommended by the panel are those who are truly credible and capable.
Past President of the Malaysian Bar Karen Cheah Yee Lynn speaks at a forum discussing the separation of powers between the Attorney General and Public Prosecutor in Kuala Lumpur. April 20, 2026—Picture by Raymond Manuel
Under the Bill, the Malaysian government proposes to no longer have its chief legal adviser, AG, decide on whether to charge or drop charges, and to instead have the PP make such decisions to strengthen prosecutorial independence.
Civil society and non-governmental organisations have however cautioned that the finer details of the Bill for this proposal needs to be improved further to truly achieve prosecutorial independence.
Former Malaysian Bar president Karen Cheah, who was a speaker at the same forum, yesterday said one of the Malaysian Bar’s biggest concerns about the Bill is the mechanism to appoint the PP and said there should be more independent persons added to the appointment panel.
“We think this appointment process and mechanism is the most important thing we are taking care of. If the root is rotten, the fruit will also be rotten. That’s why we want to fix it at the root,” she said.
At the forum titled “Separating the Powers of the Attorney General and Public Prosecutor: Does the Bill deliver independence, accountability and the rule of law?”, the three other speakers were independent policy strategy consultant and former lawyer Maha Balakrishnan; Human Rights Commission of Malaysia commissioner and former deputy public prosecutor Melissa Akhir; and Projek SAMA convener and lawyer Ngeow Chow Ying.
Recommended reading:
Syed Saddiq worries AG-PP separation Bill may not make it if no ‘middle ground’ soon, hopes done pre-GE16
Breaking up is hard to do — Malaysia still seeking ‘middle ground’ in AG-Prosecutor split
Azalina: AG and PP roles set for split after bipartisan support; parliamentary vote expected in June
‘No sugarcoating’: Azalina says govt’s proposed AG-PP split to address public concern about accountability
AG-PP split explained: Key things to know about Malaysia’s massive legal reform

Title: Food vs fuel: Survey shows what Malaysians and Thais fear most as oil jitters rise
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 21 — A new survey has found food and fuel prices topping consumer worries in Malaysia and Thailand as tensions in the Strait of Hormuz keep oil markets volatile.
The study by Rakuten Insight, conducted earlier this month with 2,106 respondents, showed both countries share an identical economic worry score of 8.09 out of 10 despite diverging concerns.
“When you dig beneath them, you find two countries experiencing the same macro pressure through completely different lenses.
“For Malaysia, it is the kitchen. For Thailand, it is the fuel pump. Same thermometer reading, completely different symptoms, and that has profound implications for any business operating across both markets,” Rakuten Insight Malaysia and Thailand country director Collin Leow said in a statement accompanying the survey results.
Food vs fuel divide
In Malaysia, 35.8 per cent of respondents cited food and groceries as their top concern, compared to 24.1 per cent who pointed to fuel despite existing subsidies.
In Thailand, 34.4 per cent were most worried about fuel and transport costs, while 26.8 per cent cited food prices.
Spending cuts deepen
The survey found consumers in both countries are cutting back on spending in response to rising costs.
It showed 63.9 per cent of Thai respondents and 52.1 per cent of Malaysians are prioritising spending cuts.
The divergence is reflected in the food service sector, where Thailand recorded a net spending intent of -42.0 compared to -22.1 in Malaysia.
The study also found 19.9 per cent of Malaysians are shifting towards locally made products as a way to cope with price increases.
